data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-16T01:29:40.857Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET migration. Let me provide what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a progression of changes around synchronous operations:

1. In EF Core 9 (/.NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing exceptions for synchronous operations by default, but you could opt back in with a configuration option.

2. In .NET 10, synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider were deprecated but could still be used with an opt-in flag (like `CosmosOptionsExtension` with `AllowSynchronousIO` or similar).


3. In .NET 11, the Cosmos DB provider completely removes sync-over-async support, so the opt-in no longer works and all synchronous LINQ operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` will throw `InvalidOperationException`. The timeline shows EF Core 9 initially removed synchronous I/O support by default, though there was a temporary workaround to re-enable it.

Since the user is on .NET 10 with that opt-in configured, upgrading to .NET 11 means losing the ability to use synchronous calls entirely — the configuration won't help anymore and everything will break.
